posts.caption:
@KateHudson has taken on a wide range of genres, but she’s still focused on pushing herself even further. When we asked Hudson what she still hopes to achieve in her career, she didn’t point to accolades—just a desire to keep pursuing roles that scare her, the same approach she brings to everything she does. ⁠
⁠
“I enter every experience like it’s new, like I don’t know how it’s going to unfold,” she explains. “I come in doing my part, which is understanding what makes me feel confident in the role and the character, but I do that so that when I’m there, I can be flexible, not rigid.” Read Hudson’s full interview at the link in our bio. ⁠
⁠

posts.caption:
Fashion may change with the tide, but swimwear tells the story. From the elegance of postwar seaside dressing to the glamour of the jet-set years, the L’OFFICIEL archives reveal how swimwear has reflected changing ideals of beauty, leisure, and luxury. As #MiamiSwimWeek returns, we look back at the silhouettes that shaped summers past—and the cultural moments that shaped them.
Beyond the beach, these images offer a snapshot of how fashion has interpreted escape across generations. Whether defined by Riviera refinement, tropical exuberance, or unapologetic glamour, each era brought its own vision of the perfect getaway. Together, they chart the evolution of resort dressing as both fantasy and cultural artifact, reminding us that what we wear on holiday often says as much about the moment as it does the destination.
Photos courtesy of the L’OFFICIEL Archive
#swimwear #resortwear #miamiswimweek2026

posts.caption:
⁠Diamonds may be a girl’s best friend, but for @SophieBilleBrahe they are something even more. “There’s something so magical about a diamond,” she tells L’OFFICIEL. “I always feel that they are borrowed; we’re passing through their lives.”⁠
⁠
After earning her master’s degree from London’s Royal College of Art, Sophie Bille Brahe returned to Denmark in 2011 to launch her eponymous jewelry label, positioning it at the intersection of craft, art, and fashion. Nature remains her greatest source of inspiration, especially the horizon where the sky meets the sea—a vision reflected in the wavy diamond and bezel setting of her signature Grand Ensemble Ocean ring.⁠
⁠
The way her jewels are displayed is equally important to Brahe, who even designed the tables for her Copenhagen store. “Jewelry is meant to be worn, and it dies a little bit when it’s just lying somewhere,” she says. “I thought, ‘how do you give something so small the right attention in a space?’ I wanted it to stand out but still represent me, and look as clean as possible.”⁠
⁠
Brahe, who prioritizes memory and inherited objects over decoration, fills her spaces with family heirlooms tied to her aristocratic past. For her New York boutique, she installed 19th-century Bertel Thorvaldsen lion sculptures from her family’s former estate—what she calls the most precious pieces in her archive, saying, “If I can’t be there every day to take care of this place, at least the lions are there to guard it.”⁠
⁠
⁠
For our May issue, Brahe opened up her archive to L’OFFICIEL, revealing the inspirations behind her creations at the link in our bio.⁠
⁠
Writer @samanthajsimon⁠
Photos courtesy of Sophie Bille Brahe ⁠
⁠
#SophieBilleBrahe

posts.caption:
@HenryZankov is back at Diane von Furstenberg! @Zankov has been named the brand’s new artistic director, marking a homecoming more than a decade after he first joined @DVF in 2014. He spent five years working under Diane von Furstenberg (@therealdvf), where he developed a deep sensitivity to color-something that would become a signature of his work. As he shared with L’OFFICIEL in an interview last year about that period, “I was always afraid of it [color]. I’m grateful for that opportunity to have somebody open my eyes when it comes to color.”
He went on to launch his namesake label in 2020 with a six-sweater capsule collection he described as “almost like an art project” and “paintings or something that you would collect.” Since then, he’s emerged as one of fashion’s most exciting new voices, winning the CFDA American Emerging Designer of the Year Award in 2024 and returning to DVF last year with an exclusive Bergdorf Goodman capsule.
Zankov will debut his first collection for the house at New York Fashion Week this September. Read more at the link in our bio.
Photos Getty Images and Zankov
#henryzankov #zankov #dvf #dianevonfurstenberg

posts.caption:
The influence of American sportswear was impossible to miss on the Paris spring runways this season—and with the #FIFAWorldCup headed to New Jersey in just a few weeks, we’ve been asking ourselves what everyone else is wondering: what does a fashion girl wear to the World Cup?
As it turns out, our March issue already explored the sportswear obsession taking over fashion across the pond, and we’re bringing that inspiration back stateside just in time for the summer’s biggest sporting event. Whether you’re a lifelong football devotee or simply there for the spectacle of a stadium filled with bold national colors and even bolder outfits, consider this your cue to lean into sporty dressing—with a fashion-week twist.
Because getting dressed for the game was always the real sport. Head to the link in our bio to see the best runway looks that prove the fashion girl and the football fan were never mutually exclusive.
Photography @heatherhazzan
Styled by @dionemdavis
#worldcup2026 #sportswear

posts.caption:
@Balenciaga dropped its Spring Summer 2026 preview collection in Mumbai last night, and the energy in the room felt different.
With Fatima Sana Sheikh, Bobby Deol, Mouni Roy, Sanya Malhotra, Malavika Mohanan, and Krithi Shetty in attendance, it felt less like a formal showcase and more like a real moment around fashion. The room was sharp, observant, and engaged.
Fatima Sana Shaikh said it best: “Balenciaga always has a chic approach and the brand is now heading towards it even more aggressively.” And that tone carried through the evening. Nothing felt loud for attention, yet everything commanded it.
What stood out was how people responded. There was curiosity, closer inspection, and genuine interest in the details. Some silhouettes felt familiar but more refined, while others brought a quiet sense of drama without trying too hard.
That is when a collection works.
Not when it demands attention, but when it earns it.
@balenciaga #Balenciaga

posts.caption:
Jacob & Co. continues to blur the line between watchmaking, sculpture, and fantasy with the Astronomia “Gates of Olympus.”
Priced at $1.4 million, the one-of-one creation draws inspiration from Greek mythology, featuring Zeus, Olympus, celestial movement, and a fully mechanical miniature world built directly into the timepiece itself.
Inside the 18K rose gold case sits a triple-axis tourbillon, over 9 carats of baguette diamonds, and an intricate Olympus scene designed as part of the movement.
More than a watch, the piece reflects how modern haute horology is increasingly moving toward collectible art and immersive storytelling.
@JacobAndCo #JacobAndCo #Luxurious #Collab

posts.caption:
Hermès Birkin and Kelly bags are increasingly being viewed as investment assets rather than just luxury accessories.
Thanks to extreme scarcity, long boutique waitlists, handcrafted production, and consistently high resale demand, certain Birkin and Kelly models now regularly sell above retail on the secondary market. In some cases, pristine or rare versions have appreciated faster than traditional assets like stocks, gold, and real estate over the past decade.
Classic colourways, exotic leathers, and limited-edition releases continue commanding the strongest premiums, with Mini Kelly and rare Birkin models often reaching multiples far above their original retail prices. Analysts say Hermès’ tightly controlled supply strategy and timeless brand positioning are major reasons behind the bags’ long-term value retention.
As luxury resale grows globally, Birkin and Kelly bags are increasingly being treated more like collectible assets, similar to fine watches, art, or rare cars, rather than traditional fashion purchases.

posts.caption:
Galeries Lafayette Mumbai has unveiled a first-of-its-kind Lacoste x Roland-Garros retail experience in India, transforming the luxury department store into an immersive tennis-inspired destination during the French Open season.
The takeover blends fashion, sport, and French luxury through themed installations, interactive spaces, window displays, and a dedicated pop-up showcasing the latest Lacoste x Roland-Garros capsule collection. Inspired by the heritage of the iconic Parisian Grand Slam, the collection features elevated ready-to-wear, accessories, and lifestyle pieces designed around modern tennis culture and French style.
The experience also includes exclusive Novak Djokovic tournament merchandise and performance-led apparel inspired by the tennis star’s on-court wardrobe, further strengthening Lacoste’s long-standing relationship with Roland-Garros.
For Galeries Lafayette Mumbai, the activation reflects a larger shift happening across India’s luxury retail market, where global brands are increasingly focusing on immersive experiences and cultural storytelling rather than traditional shopping alone.
Images: @galerieslafayetteindia

posts.caption:
#ManifestCover
In Manifest’s May-June cover interview, Rukmini Vasanth (@rukmini_vasanth) reflects on grief, love, and the kind of resilience that quietly reshapes a person over time. Beneath the poise is a life shaped by movement, loss, and emotional inheritance, experiences that continue to inform the way she understands connection and presence.
“You don’t know what life will throw at you. But as long as you’re there for each other, that’s what matters. It’s about support and presence more than anything else,” she shares.
Our latest issue is now live on Magzter. Read the full cover story from the link in bio.

posts.alt_text:
#ManifestCover

In motion, Rukmini Vasanth (@rukmini_vasanth) feels most like herself. Unhurried, intuitive, and quietly grounded.

Away from the noise, she leans into what steadies her. “A perfect day off is restful, restorative, and quietly grounding,” she shares in her cover interview with us.

That sense of stillness carries through everything she does. Even her understanding of love is simple, shaped by what she has seen up close. “You don’t know what life will throw at you. But as long as you’re there for each other, that’s what matters.”

Between movement and pause, she holds on to what is real.

Our latest issue is now live on Magzter. Read the full cover story from the link in bio.

posts.caption:
#ManifestCover
There is a quiet power to Rukmini Vasanth (@rukmini_vasanth). One that doesn’t seek attention, yet holds it effortlessly.
For Manifest’s May–June cover, she steps into an imaginative world where temple jewellery meets contemporary silhouettes, blurring the lines between memory and modernity. She embodies a woman in transition, grounded, luminous, and yet constantly becoming.
“I’m not very good at describing myself,” she says, almost in passing.
Yet, in the same breath, her choices reveal a quiet clarity of intent. “I don’t want to isolate my character. I need to understand the whole world it exists in.”
Between restraint and reinvention, stillness and movement, she exists in that rare in-between. Not fully defined, not confined. Just on the cusp of a wider sky.
Our latest issue is now live on Magzter. Read the full cover story from the link in bio.

posts.caption:
Walking through SoHo with Maurice Kamara, you get the sense that he is judging every outfit he sees. Often, he likes to ask strangers — and, increasingly, celebrities — about their outfits on camera, posting the videos on his Instagram account the People Gallery.
At its core @thepeoplegallery_ is a document of personal style. The account has featured Kim Kardashian, Michael B. Jordan and Anna Wintour. His interviews with celebrities come off as spur of the moment, but in reality, they typically require a decent amount of planning — or waiting. “A lot of people don’t know that it’s a lot of work,” Kamara told The New York Times.
But interspersed among the celebrities are video interviews with everyday people whose outfits simply happened to catch his eye. At the link in our bio, more about Kamara’s passion for fashion and how he has built his account into what it is now. Photos by @aundre

posts.caption:
Rachel Dratch, the veteran comedian beloved for her “Saturday Night Live” characters like "Debbie Downer," is finally getting her due, with her popular, mystical "Woo Woo" podcast and a return to the Broadway stage.
In late March, Dratch began her run as the Narrator in “The Rocky Horror Show” at Studio 54, a role that has earned her her second Tony nomination. (Four years ago, she was nominated for her role as Stephanie in “POTUS: Or, Behind Every Great Dumbass Are Seven Women Trying to Keep Him Alive.”)
The role of the Narrator is often performed by an older white man. But Sam Pinkleton, the show's director, envisioned Dratch in the role for a reason: “Nobody can do dry, understated, commanding humor quite like Rachel,” he said.
After she left “S.N.L.” in 2006, Dratch's career seemed to be slowing down, and she started to panic about her future. “And then I sort of decided not to,” she told The New York Times. Read more about how @raedratch not only manifested her dreams, but is exceeding them, at the link in our bio. Photo by @ok__mccausland
posts.alt_text: Photo by New York Times Fashion & Style on May 27, 2026. May be an image of one or more people, makeup, magazine and text that says ''Debbie Downer' Is Enjoying an Upswing'.

posts.caption:
On Tuesday evening, devoted fans of the Bravo series “Summer House” gathered an AMC theater to watch the first episode of a three-part reunion. The fans, who had bought one of the $70 tickets for the screening, wore themed apparel including shirts and hoodies with several of Kyle Cooke’s most popular lines: “Carl’s a mess” and “Summer should be fun.”
The show, which was once a chance to live vicariously through hot, young people in the Hamptons, shifted into more emotional topics after it was announced that cast members, Amanda Batula and West Wilson were romantically linked off-camera. Batula had recently separated from Cooke, her husband, and Wilson had dated Ciara Miller, another cast member and one of Batula’s closest friends on the show, sparking conversations around friendship breakups and romantic betrayal.
Many in the crowd seemed to share a desire to watch the reunion with other fans of the show. “Usually I’m just at home yelling at my TV, so it’s going to be nice to yell with other people this time,” one fan told our reporter @shivanigonzalez. At the link in our bio, read more of what fans thought about the "Summer House" reunion and see more photos from the screening. Photos by @kristaschlueter

posts.caption:
Miles Davis, the jazz legend and style innovator who would have turned 100 on May 26, remains for many people the pre-eminent avatar of cool. And while Davis’s greatest legacy is musical, he also cut a distinctive image over the course of his five-decade career. (Davis died of pneumonia in 1991, at 65.) His style shifted alongside his sound, but he had his touchstones — face-obscuring sunglasses and ticket-magnet sports cars among them.
For Davis’s centennial, our writers reflected on the specific elements of the trumpeter’s public image that made him a genius of cool. From his fast cars to his huge shades, learn about all the things that made Miles Miles at the link in our bio. Photos by Michael Ochs Archives/Getty Images, David Redfern/Redferns, Getty, Christian Rose/Roger Viollet, via Getty Images, Mark Patiky/Condé Nast, via Getty Images

Some very powerful professional women do not use them, preferring clothing with pockets and/or brief cases. Is the age of the handbag over?"
Fashion may be at a pivot point when it comes to handbags. At the link in our bio, @vvfriedman explains the trend. Photo by Gotham/GC Images

posts.caption:
As booksellers try to figure out how to market audiobooks in person, Audible, the audiobook giant owned by Amazon, staged a pop-up bookstore — without any physical books.
The Audible Story House, a three-story emporium on the Lower East Side of Manhattan, has an event space for craft nights and author panels and a cafe selling matcha lattes and branded merch. The shop also has two large listening lounges equipped with surround-sound, listening booths where individuals or small groups can sample the audiobooks and a listening bar where patrons can consult a “story tender” to get personalized recommendations from a list of titles available on Audible.
“People want not only to have IRL experiences, they want things that are tangible and not just digital,” said James Finn, Audible’s global head of brand and content marketing.
Some independent booksellers have shifted their sales strategies in the hopes of winning over the audiobook consumer — and staying competitive against giants like Audible. Are bookless bookstores a solution? Tap the link in our bio to read the full story. Photos by @sethdcaplan

posts.caption:
If you have any of these bags, please step forward… #KarlLagerfeld was known for his larger-than-life shows, and the spectacle didn’t just stop at the sets, but translated into his absurd yet highly collectible bags.
Think of the iconic hula hoop bag from Spring 2013, which Karl joked was designed for the beach so you could stick it into the sand and hang your towel from it (which fyi someone is currently selling on eBay for £145K.) Then there was the supermarket show, complete with milk carton bags and the #Chanel chain basket, or the Spring 2019 flap bag filled with sand.
The bags became souvenirs from those fantasy worlds and now #MatthieuBlazy is continuing that tradition, turning handbags into collectible objects that blur the line between fashion and art.
Which one is your favourite?
posts.alt_text: Photo by umar on May 27, 2026. May be an image of magazine, gown, dress and text that says 'Does Everything in Fashion Need To Be a Reference?'.

posts.caption:
References and nostalgia are important and a large part of a brand’s visual language, but a reference stops being meaningful the moment it becomes a copy. The strongest ones take an idea and re-code it under different cultural, political, and digital conditions. #DanielRoseberry does particularly well, where #ElsaSchiaparelli’s surrealist influences are turned into something contemporary rather than nostalgic.
Digital culture has flattened time and made everything instantly searchable, and comparable, making pure “newness” even harder to define. So is referencing the only way forward? No, but it is one of the most effective ways to communicate in today’s saturated visual landscape. These days originality rarely means creating something entirely from nothing
So what do you think makes a good reference in fashion?
posts.alt_text: Photo by umar on May 28, 2026. May be an image of purse and text that says 'ሀቢት What Is the Craziest Bag Chanel Has Ever Made?'.

posts.caption:
We’ve seen #JonathanAnderson continue to use references that carry emotional memory, whether that’s art, ceramics, craft… and now children’s illustrations!
His latest #Dior collection (first teased in November 25) features #TheVeryHungryCaterpillar by Eric Carle. It’s one of the most universally recognisable children’s books, instantly familiar across generations. For many including myself, it was one of the first books I encountered as a child. It’s fun to see Jonathan Anderson continue to mix Dior’s couture sensibilities with playful and whimsical references. The result feels nostalgic, ironic, emotional, and unserious, elevating childhood memory into collectible fashion.
posts.alt_text: Photo by umar on May 28, 2026. May be an image of purse and text that says 'ሀቢት What Is the Craziest Bag Chanel Has Ever Made?'.

posts.caption:
When we think of archival fashion, the conversation is often dominated by the great Western maisons, but seeing #BellaHadid recently wear archival pieces from #ElieSaab, #ZuhairMurad, and #GeorgesChakra feels like a long-overdue reminder of how influential the work coming out of Lebanon was, and still is.
Lebanese designers really helped define the visual language of 2000s glamour at a time when much of fashion was moving toward minimalism. Their #HauteCouture embodied extravagance rooted in skills, passed down over generations across the Middle East.
(Images courtesy of Rani Fawaz/ Yasmine Diba / Bella Hadid / Elie Saab / Diggzy / Zuhair Murad / Getty / Backgrid )
posts.alt_text: Photo by umar on May 28, 2026. May be an image of purse and text that says 'ሀቢት What Is the Craziest Bag Chanel Has Ever Made?'.

posts.caption:
@balenciaga’s new #ManoloBlahnik collaboration makes perfect sense for #PierpaoloPiccioli’s evolving vision for the house. It shows where #Balenciaga is headed after years defined by irony, provocation, and streetwear. Signalling to a sense of couture and refinement, we haven’t really seen in a while.
By collaborating with one of the most revered shoe designers, I love how Piccioli references the shared Spanish heritage of #CristóbalBalenciaga and @manoloblahnik which makes the partnership feel rooted in something deeper than celeb hype or marketing.
While theres still exaggerated sneakers for the fanbase established under #Demna, a pair of hand made crystal satin slingbacks seems to be the perfect welcome back for the customer searching for glamour and femininity once again.
What do you think of the collab?

Esha Singh set a world record (and junior world record) in 25m pistol shooting with a score of 43, clinching a Gold medal at the International Shooting Sport Federation (ISSF) World Cup Rifle in Munich on May 27. She surpassed the previous record of 42 set by South Korea's Kim Ye-ji.
At just 21, Esha is a three-time world championship and double Asian Games medallist. She has represented India at several global platforms, including the Paris Olympics 2024.

Major Abhilasha Barak, an Indian peacekeeper serving at the United Nations’ mission in Lebanon, has been awarded the prestigious United Nations Military Gender Advocate of the Year (2025) Award for her outreach efforts with women and girls during her deployment in the West Asian nation.
India’s Permanent Mission to the UN said, “Proud to announce that Major Abhilasha Barak has been awarded the 2025 @UN Military Gender Advocate of the Year Award. She is being recognised for her outreach and community engagement activities for women and adolescent girls and gender sensitisation training for peacekeepers."

posts.caption:
At 40, Bengaluru's Ashwini Ganapathi has made history as the first Indian woman to complete the Asia Trail Master (ATM) Grandmaster Quest — one of the toughest endurance trail-running challenges.
The Quest requires runners to complete six races of 70km or more within TWO YEARS across different countries and terrains.
Ashwini began the challenge in 2024 with races across Malaysia, Japan, and Vietnam, including multiple 100-mile ultras.
Her final race in the two-year challenge came at Merapoh Rainforest Trail in Pahang, Malaysia, on May 1. She navigated a gruelling 164-km course with 3,700 metres of elevation gain through rainforest terrain.
She completed the run in 28 hours and 33 minutes, finishing 5th overall among 146 starters and 2nd among 27 women participants.
Source: The Times of India
Visit www.shethepeople.tv for more.

posts.caption:
Not every designer just creates clothes. Some spend decades building a language - one that speaks of India’s past, present, and everything in between… quietly, yet profoundly. ✨
Think of Ritu Kumar not just as a name on a label… but as a living archive of Indian craft. A designer who understood before anyone else that heritage wasn’t something to preserve behind glass - it was something to wear, move in, and celebrate.
The heritage weaves that carry centuries of artisan work in every motif and border. The iconic vintage golds that feel like they were pulled from a royal trousseau and made entirely new. The classic bridal red - never overdone, always undeniably powerful, deeply and timelessly Indian. The velvets that feel regal in a way only Ritu Kumar can - draping the body like they were always meant to be there. 💫
It’s never just about the outfit… It’s about the feeling of wearing something that has this much history, this much intention, and this much love stitched into every inch of it.
Because the most enduring bridal fashion isn’t always what’s newest… It’s the work where craft, culture, and beauty simply fall perfectly into place.
For over five decades, Ritu Kumar has dressed India’s brides, its runways, and its most memorable moments - and in 2026, that legacy feels more relevant than ever.
So if you could wear one Ritu Kumar creation on your wedding day… which world would you choose to step into? 🤍

posts.caption:
Kashmir doesn’t just host weddings.
It becomes them - the mist that rises without asking, the mountains that sit in the background like they’ve always known this moment was coming, and a valley so quietly magnificent that it doesn’t compete with the love story in front of it. It simply holds it.
And that is exactly what it did for Simran and Sayam.
And then there was them. ❤️
But what made this celebration impossible to look away from was how completely the setting became part of the feeling - bubbles catching the pale Kashmir light, a bride in deep red standing out against skies that felt infinite, and two people facing each other like the whole valley had gone quiet just for them.
Some weddings are set against beautiful places.
And some stay with you because the place and the people became one thing entirely - the mist, the mountains, the moment, and a love story told in the most breathtaking valley in the country. 🌟
Couple: Simran & Sayam
Location: Kashmir
Photos Courtesy: @fffilms.in

Think of choosing a saree over a lehenga not just as a style preference… but as a statement. A choice that says you know exactly what you want, and it looks like six yards of something extraordinary.
The saree draped over a long veil that makes every entrance feel like it belongs in a film. The modern drape styled with a corset blouse and pearl shoulder chains - tradition, completely reimagined. The silk saree that carries the weight of heritage in every thread and catches the light in a way nothing else can. The vintage gold border that doesn’t need embellishment to be breathtaking - it simply is. 💫
It’s never just about the outfit… It’s about the way a saree moves with you, wraps around you, and makes you feel more yourself than anything else ever could.
Because the most unforgettable bridal looks in 2026 aren’t always the ones with the most volume… They’re the ones where the drape, the blouse, and the woman wearing it simply fall perfectly into place.
The saree is having its biggest bridal moment yet - and these brides are all the proof you need.
So if you had to choose between a lehenga and a saree for your wedding day… which one would actually feel like you? 🤍

posts.caption:
Fashion styling is a job. Not a hobby. Not a passion project. Not something people do on the side because they love clothes.
It is a profession that takes years to build. The eye. The contacts. The knowledge of how the industry actually works across every sector, every brief, every type of client. That does not come from loving fashion. It comes from years of showing up, doing the work and figuring it out mostly alone.
And yet it is still one of the most undervalued roles in the creative industry.
Stylists are asked to work for free. For credit. For exposure. For a rate that does not come close to reflecting the skill, the hours or the experience involved. And somewhere along the way the industry decided that was normal. That stylists should be grateful for the opportunity. That loving what you do means you should not expect to be paid properly for it.
We are past that.
Behind every iconic image, every red carpet moment, every campaign that stopped you mid-scroll, there was a stylist. Building the looks, interpreting the brief, managing the pressure, absorbing the chaos so nobody else had to. Often without a credit. Sometimes without a proper rate.
That changes when stylists know their worth, charge it without apology and stop accepting the jobs that treat their career like a favour.
➡️➡️ If you are a working fashion stylist who is ready to do exactly that, comment STUDIO and we will send you the free trial link to The Studio of Stylist Elixir. Rate guides. Contracts. Portfolio tools. Masterclasses every Monday. Everything you need to build a career that reflects what you are actually worth.
Save this. Send it to a stylist who needs to hear it.

The most talented stylist in the room is not always the one with the biggest career. That has nothing to do with ability.
Talent gets you in. It is not what levels you up. The business side is where most careers break, the rates, the pitching, the contracts, the part talent alone cannot carry.
Undercharging becomes a habit. There is never a plan beyond the next booking. And doing it all alone is not noble, it is just slow.
Stuck is not a verdict on your talent. It is a stage, and stages can be moved through.
✦ Fix what is missing around the talent, and the talent that was always there finally gets to move. ✦

You are turning down work you would have begged for two years ago. Your rate has not moved in three seasons while your work has got better every single one. You are the most experienced person on set and still being booked, and billed, like an assistant. The diary is full. The bank balance is not. Here is the truth nobody says out loud. You will not grow out of this on your own. Not because you are not good enough, you clearly are, but because talent was never the thing holding you back. The business was. The plan was. The room of people who have already done it was.

So picture six months from now. You quote your new rate without your voice shaking, and they say yes. Your diary is full of the work you actually want, the work that builds the name you are after. The right clients do not just pay better. They change the entire shape of your career.
Every client you say yes to tells the industry what kind of fashion stylist you are. Take the work that drains you and underpays you, and that becomes the career you are known for. So the goal was never more clients. It was the right ones. The ones who pay properly, respect your creative direction, and book you again. A handful of those is worth more than a calendar full of the wrong ones.
Here is the part nobody likes to hear. Dream clients do not find you by accident. They find you when your portfolio shows the work you want more of, your rates signal your level, and your positioning makes it obvious who you are for. That is built on purpose, not luck.
That is exactly what the six weeks are for. The Fashion Mentorship Program rebuilds your portfolio, your rates, and your positioning, so the clients who actually serve your career are the ones coming to you.
✦ You do not need more clients. You need the right ones. ✦
You leave with a portfolio that books, rates you can hold, and positioning that attracts the right clients and filters out the wrong ones. Weekly group sessions and private 1:1 calls the whole way through.

posts.caption:
Growing a fashion styling business is the part almost nobody talks about, because styling alone has a ceiling.
There are only so many days in the week and so much one person can charge. Real growth is not a higher day rate. It is moving from stylist to creative director, building brand partnerships instead of one-off bookings, bringing in a team that works under your name, reading where the industry is heading before it shifts, and never relying on one income stream. And at the very top, building a business with a name and a value beyond you, that could one day run, or even sell, without you in the room.
✦ Getting booked is one skill. Growing past your own two hands is another entirely. ✦
This is the final part of a series on building a fashion styling career, from breaking in, to going solo, to growing the business.
💚 For every stylist who has hit the ceiling of their own time
💚 For anyone ready to build something bigger than one day rate
💚 For the working stylist thinking past the next booking

posts.caption:
@_pattaraphan_ is one of those brands that makes you pause mid-scroll. Founded by Pattaraphan “Nok” Salirathavibhaga, the Bangkok-based label sits right at the intersection of sculpture and fine jewelry—clean, minimal, but with just enough edge to feel personal. Every piece is made locally in Bangkok, grounded in Thai craftsmanship but designed with a global, contemporary eye.
Launched in 2018, the brand was built to fill a gap: fine jewelry that didn’t feel traditional or overly polished, but still carried weight. Genderless, intentional, and quietly distinctive.
And clearly, people are paying attention. From FKA Twigs to Bella Hadid, it’s become one of those insider brands that signals taste without trying too hard.
Understated, but not forgettable.
#Pattaraphan #FineJewelry #JewelryDesign #Minimalism #Bangkok

posts.caption:
Major fashion houses cycle through creative directors for a few core reasons: shifting aesthetics, evolving philosophies, financial pressure, and the constant churn of consumer demand. At a brand like Gucci, that tension has turned the role of creative director into something much bigger than a traditional couturier.
We’ve seen this pattern before in different forms across the industry: moments of extreme cultural saturation that feel successful in real time, but become harder to defend once the cycle moves on. Attention alone doesn’t rebuild a business model, and it certainly doesn’t guarantee loyalty—either from long-standing Gucci customers or from the newer audience drawn in by spectacle.
Read the latest on Substack: The Citizen’s Poste | posteposteposte.com
#Gucci #Demna #LaFamiglia #FashionCritic #TomFord

posts.caption:
The Bottega Veneta Tosca Bag is honestly one of the best examples of why craftsmanship still matters in luxury fashion.
The bag is made entirely by hand without a single traditional stitch or seam. Instead, one artisan constructs the entire piece using a three-dimensional version of Bottega’s signature intrecciato weaving technique—weaving hundreds of feet of leather strips directly into the structure of the bag itself.
A single Tosca can require nearly 500 feet of leather and up to five full days of handwork to complete.
And because each one is woven individually from start to finish, no two bags are ever completely identical. Less “accessory,” more wearable sculpture.
This is the kind of craftsmanship fashion people are talking about when they say true luxury can’t be mass-produced.
#BottegaVeneta #LuxuryFashion #LeatherCraftsmanship #Intrecciato #TheCitizensPoste
posts.alt_text: Photo by The Citizen’s Poste on May 28, 2026. May be an image of jewelry and text that says 'This Bangkok-Based Based Fine Jewelry Bangkok Brand is Insanely Beautiful'.

posts.caption:
By the way, the wristwatch was invented by women. For centuries, wristwatches were actually considered jewelry—delicate, decorative, and almost exclusively feminine—while men relied on pocket watches for “serious” timekeeping. The earliest recorded wristwatch commission dates back to 1810, when Caroline Murat, Napoleon’s younger sister, ordered a bracelet watch from Breguet.
So yes, while male watchmakers engineered the mechanics, women were the ones who transformed the wristwatch into a wearable fashion category in the first place.
Ironically, men didn’t fully embrace wristwatches until military conflicts in the late 19th and early 20th centuries made pocket watches impractical.
Fashion history stays humbling people daily.
#WatchHistory #FashionHistory #Breguet #LuxuryWatches #TheCitizensPoste

posts.caption:
It’s Simon Porte Jacquemus’ world and we’re honestly just living in it. What makes Jacquemus so dominant right now is that the brand doesn’t just sell clothes, it sells feeling: Mediterranean fantasy, exaggerated romance, humor, intimacy, sunshine, nostalgia. Everything feels emotional before it feels commercial.
And somehow, Jacquemus has managed to turn that emotion into one of the smartest luxury marketing machines in fashion. Viral runway moments, giant surrealist installations, hyper-shareable campaigns, celebrity casting, raw personal storytelling—all while still maintaining the feeling of an independent dream rather than a corporate luxury machine.
That balance is incredibly hard to fake.
Fashion people love to talk about “world-building,” but Jacquemus is one of the few brands actually doing it successfully.
#Jacquemus #SimonPorteJacquemus #FashionMarketing #LuxuryFashion #TheCitizensPoste

posts.caption:
This human artist creates oil paintings that literally look like dreams and I need one in my life immediately—and no, they’re not AI.
@diango.hernandez is a Cuban artist and designer whose work constantly gets mistaken for digital art online because the paintings are that surreal, but it’s all done by hand. Hernández blends his background in industrial design with traditional oil painting techniques, creating these hyper-precise compositions that still somehow feel emotional and unstable at the same time.
Honestly one of those artists that makes you stare at a painting for ten minutes trying to figure out how anyone could think human art could ever be replaced.
Literally insane.
#ContemporaryArt #DiangoHernandez #OilPainting #ArtWorld #TheCitizensPoste

A viral song today arrives already broken into parts: the hook for recall, the lyric for captions, the beat for choreography, the mood for someone else’s edit.
In India, musicians are now writing for the song and the scroll, where a few seconds can push a track harder than an old-school rollout.
The shift has made discovery more democratic and more chaotic. Forgotten tracks return through memes and wedding videos. Official audio can turn clips into royalties.
A song can travel faster than any label budget and still risk becoming just another piece of content moving at algorithm speed.

posts.caption:
The male body, once allowed a certain laziness, is now expected to show its working. At some point, getting hotter stopped sounding fun.
Looksmaxxing sits at that extreme end. It is male vanity after it has been given a scoring system. In a culture where status, masculinity and identity can feel unstable, looksmaxxing offers the illusion of control.
The manosphere gives that promise its sharper ideological edge, folding self-improvement into fear of rejection, romantic inadequacy, peer comparison and the belief that appearance determines social worth.
Terms like “low-tier man”, “subhuman” and “Giga Chad” reduce human identity into rankings and visual metrics.
“Bone-smashing is dangerous and has no scientific basis whatsoever. Mewing is wildly overhyped; there is no robust clinical evidence it meaningfully reshapes adult craniofacial structure,” says Dr Macrene Alexiades.
Head to the link in bio to read why the future of looksmaxxing may not be its most brutal version, but its most ordinary-looking one.
By Ria Bhatia (@riabbhatia)

posts.caption:
Collagen powder has slipped from supplement jars into smoothie bowls and beauty cabinets, sold as shortcuts to glow. But hype does not equal efficacy.
Experts point out that collagen does not rebuild skin. It provides building blocks and signals that can support collagen production.
Marine, bovine, and vegan collagen each come with trade-offs, but transparency and consistency matter more than marketing. Visible results require eight to twelve weeks of disciplined use.
Read more at the link in bio
By Ria Bhatia (@riabbhatia)
#collagen #skincare #beautytrends #wellnessindia #supplements

posts.caption: In most animal species, nature tends to maintain a roughly equal number of males and females because this balance maximizes reproductive success over generations. However, in many situations, populations may become female-biased because females are more important for increasing population size; a single male can reproduce with multiple females, but each female can produce only a limited number of offspring. In addition, males in many species face higher mortality due to fighting, competition, risk-taking, and greater visibility to predators. Some animals, such as bees and ants, naturally produce overwhelmingly female populations because only a few males are needed for reproduction. Thus, evolution does not intentionally “favor” females, but biological and environmental pressures often make female-heavy populations more advantageous for survival and recovery.

posts.caption:
Tommy Hilfiger’s Liverpool FC chapter is entering its next era. fronted by Virgil van Dijk, Florian Wirtz, Alexander Isak, Dominik Szoboszlai, Cody Gakpo and more, the new summer 2026 campaign captures football at the exact moment sport, fashion and celebrity culture collide.
shot around Anfield by Alisdair McLellan, the campaign reworks Tommy’s classic Americana through the lens of modern football culture: oversized tailoring, cable knit polos, linen blazers and perfectly styled tunnel walk vibes. it’s prep, but with the confidence of the most watched athletes in the world.
the smartest part of the campaign is that it understands what football has become. players aren’t just representing clubs anymore, they’re shaping fashion, internet culture and global style at the same time. and Tommy Hilfiger clearly knows it.
#TFO #tommyhilfiger #liverpoolFC #fashion #football
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by THE FASHION OBSERVER on April 15, 2026 tagging @vestiaireco. May be an image of leather, purse, grocery bag and text that says 'if you've been waiting to hunt, this preloved sale is kind of of the moment tfo'.

posts.caption:
AOJIEROU isn’t making clothes for the timid, and nor is the collaboration with @zhangranze . the Antwerp-based designer is building emotional armour, somewhere between a Victorian romance, a war film, and the hottest person you’ve ever seen on the street at 3 a.m.
known for blending military structures with delicate silhouettes, AOJIEROU’s work explores protection, fear, beauty, masculinity, femininity, and the tension between all of them. bulletproof gowns, sculptural tailoring, medieval armour references, and silhouettes that look like they survived the apocalypse beautifully.
after gaining attention from fashion insiders and even BLACKPINK, the brand has become one of the most exciting emerging names to watch, not because it follows trends, but because it feels completely detached from them.
#TFO #fashion #AOJIEROU #designer #blackpink

posts.caption:
when did Formula 1 become fashion’s biggest runway?
and when did luxury brands stop sponsoring the sport… and start wanting to own the culture around it?
Gucci and Alpine Formula 1 Team have just announced a long-term partnership starting in 2027, with the team set to become Gucci Racing Alpine Formula 1 Team. but is this really just a sponsorship? or the beginning of a completely new era where fashion houses become part of the sport itself?
#TFO #GUCCI #F1

posts.caption:
Look at anyone’s online bio today, and you’ll find a grocery list of skills and accomplishments. No one’s just one thing.
On ‘The Nod’ team masthead itself you’ll see an audience growth manager who runs a bookstagram. Our social media editor at work goes home to make comedic reels. And our fashion writer, we’re convinced, should have her own YouTube channel. It’s as 2026 as it can be.
Of course, our cover girl Samantha Ruth Prabhu (@samantharuthprabhuoffl) represents everything we’re talking about—movie star, producer, serial entrepreneur, chatty podcaster. A multi-hyphenate in the true sense of the term.
As The Nod turns two, all this month we will feature an impressive array of all-rounders: a brand strategist with an alter ego, a BAFTA-nominated actor dabbling in prog rock, a hustler with more jobs than days in a week, an architect who’s a drag queen…
But are these side hustles a great work-life blend to save us all from burnout or just faux-inspirational tales designed as coping mechanism? Is it a way to ensure that we’re relevant in a chaotic time where job security is dismal, technology is throwing new realities our way, and the world is at war? Or is it our unsatiable curiosity that is leading us there? This May, join us as we explore ‘The Age of the Poly-Professional’.
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by The Nod on May 21, 2026 tagging @gouravadarsh, and @jubinav. May be an image of one or more people, makeup, people smiling, poster, magazine and text that says 'n ft. Adarsh Gourav'.

posts.caption:
For Adarsh Gourav (@gouravadarsh)—the BAFTA-nominated actor who had the dream debut of playing a younger Shah Rukh Khan in ‘My Name is Khan’ (2010) and has, in the last 15 years, worked with bucket-list filmmakers like Zoya Akhtar, Bejoy Nambiar, Ramin Bahrani, and Arjun Varain Singh—music has always been his first love. It’s a love seeded in his childhood, one that has stuck despite often being unrequited, through rejection, failure, and harsh realisations. It has been his Everest, and it has given him everything he has today; he would likely not be on screen if it weren’t for being “noticed” while singing ‘Vande Mataram’ at the Kala Ghoda Arts Festival as a teenager, which really put him on the path to auditioning for films.
“I’ve always wanted to make music. I wanted to be a playback singer,” he says now, relaying a parallel life story that’s stayed under wraps until very recently. “And now I’m just using my street cred as an actor to push this side of myself. And why not? I hope they’ll think: ‘Acting achhi kar leta hai toh ye bhi theek thaak kar hi leta hoga.’”
At the link in bio, @_niddee_ chats with the polymath about the rare vantage of enjoying the best of both worlds.

posts.caption:
Growing up in Chennai as the youngest of three siblings, acting wasn’t part of the plan for model student Samantha Ruth Prabhu (@samantharuthprabhuoffl). During her gap year after college, the then 21-year-old dabbled in modelling and ended up at a film audition for kicks. “I thought if I do get it, I’ll do it for a year and then go back to studying,” she says. “The idea was to do it temporarily, never make it a career. I’m sure my teachers were disappointed when I became an actor. I think some of them still are,” she laughs.
We’re not the first to ask her what her alternative career would be, but this time she has an answer: “I’m doing it now,” she says with excitement. “I think this entrepreneurial journey is what I’ve always wanted to do,” says the serial entrepreneur, whose work straddles films, beauty, wellness, fashion, food, and sport, all at once.
For our second anniversary issue, @chloechou13 flew to Hyderabad to chat with our cover star. Full interview at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
The internet is currently going through what can only be described as a Y2K tech relapse. People are carrying digital cameras again, willingly untangling wired earphones, and romanticising translucent gadgets like it is 1999. And nobody is falling for minimalism again.
Somewhere in the middle of this revival sits cyberdecking, a form of rebellion where people are building tiny custom computers out of random objects that spark joy, instead of buying another grey gadget from a tech giant. Our favourite cyberdecking creator? 30-year-old Trushitha Narla(@mewtru).
At the link in bio, @sheyakurian chats with Narla on the Etsy-fication of gadgets.

posts.caption:
Coffee culture in Delhi-NCR is in a very specific phase right now. Cafes are no longer just places to refuel and caffeinate; they’ve become the city’s preferred third spaces, where entire afternoons (and evenings) disappear between work calls, catchups with friends, first dates, and even solo hangs.
The coffee itself has kept pace. There’s storytelling, fermentation experiments, new flavour notes to taste and trivia to learn, minus the intimidating energy of a full-blown coffee geek. And The Drop(@thedrop.del) by Passcode Hospitality(@passcodehospitality)—from the team behind Saz (Mumbai), Jamun (Delhi, Goa, and Hyderabad), and PCO (Delhi)—fits squarely in this lane.
Read @geetikasachdev’s full review of the cafe at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
You are absolutely and completely wrong.
Heritage survives only when people actually WANT to wear it. Which means it has to become fashion first. That is how textile cultures survive — through reinvention, adaptation, and changing with people.
Also, “restraint”?
Which part of Indian textiles suggests restraint exactly?
The colours?
The zari?
The brocades?
The regional drapes?
The fact that sari drapes changed across states depending on labour, climate, movement, and lifestyle?
Indian textiles were never static museum objects. They evolved constantly because people changed them constantly.
If nobody reinvented craft, adapted silhouettes, changed drapes, experimented with colour, or made textiles fashionable for newer generations, most Indian crafts would have died under mill-made polyester decades ago.
Invention is not the corruption of heritage.
It is the reason heritage survives.
Please do some actual research before posting something this historically unaware about Indian textiles, their evolution, their function, and their place in contemporary life.

posts.caption:
Hi, I’m Veronica 👋🏼 a sewing pattern designer based in Melbourne, Australia.
I create patterns, tools and resources that help you sew with understanding.
My work focuses on how fabrics behave, how garments are constructed, and how sewing connects us to the history of what we wear.
Sewing Patterns
My patterns are designed for versatility, wearability and fun. They include the Hazel, Freya, Odette, Audrey, Hera, Cordelia and the Sleeve Studio — each combining comfort with thoughtful design details. You’ll also find The Noelle, a free beginner-friendly pattern that introduces my approach to fit and construction.
The Paper Doll
A print-at-home paper doll designed to help you visualise and play with my sewing patterns. You can cut it out and colour it yourself, or use the pre-filled versions featuring my fabric prints and colour palettes. The set includes curvier model options and three skin tones to choose from.
The Conscious Consumer eBook
A practical guide to fabrics, sustainability and sewing with intention. It helps you understand fibre origins, weave structures and how to make long-lasting choices when you sew or buy clothes.
Digital Sewing Tools
Two paid calculators designed for precision:
• Shirring Calculator – works out fabric shrinkage and elastic thread length.
• GSM Calculator – estimates fabric weight and drape.
Available individually or as a discounted bundle.
My PDF sewing patterns are stocked digitally through @fabricgodmother , @spotlightstores , @thefoldline , @joans.finefabric and @thedrapersdaughter .
Fabric and Accessories
You can shop my fabric designs on @spoonflower and find my illustrated phone cases at @thedairy .
Everything I create is designed to help you sew with confidence, understanding and creativity.
For all enquires email us at hello@veronicatuckerthelabel.com
posts.alt_text: Photo by Veronica Tucker the Label on May 27, 2026. May be an image of poster and text that says 'The Goddess GoddessProje Project The Three Fates The three women who controlled birth, life and death. All through cloth. veronica veronicatucker tucker'.

posts.caption:
They spin the thread. They measure it. They cut it.
Three women have governed every life that has ever been lived and held it in their hands. Not even the King of the Gods could change what they had decided ⚡️
It was always women holding the thread.
The Three Fates is part of the Goddess Project - a series tracing the history of women, cloth, and power across every culture that has ever existed.
New to the series? Start with The First Makers: Ancient Mediterranean & Near East which is also available as a podcast episode on The Culture of Cloth 🎧
posts.alt_text: Photo by Veronica Tucker the Label on October 23, 2025.

posts.caption:
Three months ago I started drafting a sleeve I couldn’t stop thinking about.
The gigot sleeve has come back twice in history: the 1890s, when women were cycling and going to university and moving through the world on their own terms for the first time. And the 1980s, when Diana’s wedding gown set the template and Dynasty amplified it.
Both times it meant that women were claiming more space.
I wanted to build one properly. So I made one with a shirring system that works in any fabric and a cuff that fits every arm. Graded to size 26 and a manual that actually explains the engineering behind it.
I made mine in silk dupioni (the same weight as Sarah’s ballgown in Labyrinth) because if you’re going to take up room, it should feel like magic 🪄
The Isolde sleeve is here. AUD $15 standalone, or bundle with the Audrey dress for AUD $40. Link in bio.
posts.alt_text: Photo by Veronica Tucker the Label on October 23, 2025.

posts.caption:
Lately I’ve been thinking a lot about how women have been required to multitask throughout history and the impact that this has on our creativity.
On one hand, we’re able to do a million things at once and on the other hand, we have to do a million things at once.
What comes to mind when I think of this topic are usually snippets of Mr Bennet retiring to his reading room and closing the door, away from his nagging wife and wild daughters.
Imagine if, as women, we had the same luxury? And throughout history? How much further would we have come? Politically, creatively, financially.
Reading ‘A Room of One’s Own’ by Virginia Woolf really opened up my mind to this topic. I highly recommend reading.
posts.alt_text: Photo by Veronica Tucker the Label on October 23, 2025.

posts.caption:
The oldest written story in human history is about a woman and what she wears.
Before Homer or the Bible or almost everything we call civilisation, the first makers were here.
This is Edition I of The First Makers: a series inside the Goddess Project tracing the weaving goddesses who presided over making across every culture, independently, without contact with each other.
This edition: Ancient Mediterranean & Near East.
Uttu. Inanna. Neith. Hathor. Isis. Athena.
Six goddesses over 5000 years of record all with one argument: making was never just craft. It was power, sovereignty, magic, and the measure of civilisation.
The Culture of Cloth podcast episode on the First Makers drops this week 🔗 link in bio.
Save this. The series continues.
posts.alt_text: Photo by Veronica Tucker the Label on October 23, 2025.

posts.caption:
It’s been an incredible 12 months of growth, but it’s time for me to slow down.
I feel like I’ve been stretching myself pretty thin to make sure I post something every single day. But the time has come to give myself some space to let the deeper work find its way out.
Thank you for being here, I’m excited to bring you even deeper and  considered, creative work 🫶🏼
posts.alt_text: Photo by Veronica Tucker the Label on October 23, 2025.

posts.caption:
“I don’t want to understand everyone; I want a world where I live and let others live. In New York City, I was staying in the middle of a huge building, surrounded by people of different races and religions, and there was no tension whatsoever. Sloterdijk even talks about how we need to learn a certain kind of discretion. That’s what bothers me — the idea that I have to understand your world. How am I supposed to understand your world when neither you understand it nor I understand my own? I’m very superficial in that regard. You have a neighbor — establish some kind of order, with basic respect, and don’t push yourself onto them, nor let them push themselves onto you. I take great pleasure in discretion. Maybe that comes with age.”
For the May Issue of Vogue Adria, we spoke with @SlavojZizeks, one of today’s most renowned philosophers, about the future of the left, the dangers facing modern society, one night stands, and revolution.
Photo: @juergentellerstudio
Creative Partner: @doviledrizyte
Interview: @dazedsparkle
Editor-in-Chief @milandjacic
Post Production: Louwre Erasmus for QuickFix
Artwork 'Z GLAVO SKOZI ZID' by @___dnlm___
Vogue Adria, May 2026

posts.caption: Any update on missing magazines and blue books: I placed two orders for the Connor Storrie covers with the special edition blue books months ago. A month and a half ago I was told that they shipped to my forwarding service. A week ago I finally received tracking for one of the two orders but it shows that it was “returned to sender” three weeks ago. What is happening with my order? I’ve never had an issue with the forwarding service and know friends who have received their order through the same service. I’ve also sent multiple emails on this. I’m happy to pay for shipping twice for my orders to be sent to a friend in Europe instead or directly to the US.

posts.caption: For the most part, high-low brand collaborations are a winning formula. The smaller brand gains exposure and scale, the more established one accrues cultural cachet, and customers can access their favorite brands at a lower price. But for those that have built their reputations on strong sustainability credentials, linking up with fast fashion players can come with serious risk. Mango, H&M, Topshop, and Asics are among those eyeing collaborations with sustainable names, but how can designers make sure their values align? Contributing editor @SophieBenson unpacks the new playbook for sustainable brands seeking collaborations at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
Nostalgia is often not cultural preservation.
It is cultural risk aversion.
A lot of brands, media companies, and even artists flex nostalgia because inventing new desire is far harder than remixing and inheriting.
That’s why so much contemporary branding feels emotionally dead despite being visually competent. It references culture instead of contributing to it.
Healthy cultures produce forward movements.
Status quo are the declining cultures endlessly referencing themselves.

posts.caption: @HenryZankov has been named Artistic Director of Diane Von Furstenberg, a new role at the company. The appointment follows CEO Graziano di Boni’s 2025 move to bring the DVF business back in-house after four years of outsourcing its management to Chinese licensee Glamel. “Henry brings fresh energy, a strong point of view, and cultural relevance for a new generation to discover DVF,” he said in a statement. It’s a homecoming for Zankov, who worked at the brand when the Scottish designer Jonathan Saunders was creative director in the mid-2010s. He’ll make his debut in September during New York Fashion Week. Learn more at the link in bio.

When Shreeya Patel and Unmesh Rajendran began planning their wedding in Italy, it was not their first choice. They had initially pictured Kerala, but the rain was relentless. Then, Sicily entered the picture. The sangeet was held in a garden flanked by arched turrets and ponds and Patel built her look around a sequinned oversized vintage Dior jacket. The following morning began with a roaring baraat with dhols and a DJ truck that rolled through the orangery within the venue. Rajendran wore Matsya for the ceremony and while Patel had been “determined to wear colour,” she eventually chose an ivory lace Sabyasachi Couture ensemble. To see their wedding in detail, head to the link in bio.

posts.caption:
The women’s world number one @ArynaSabalenka understands that tennis can be opera: full of triumph and despair, grace and slapstick, sin and atonement. The 28-year-old has been at the top of her sport for the last year and a half, with a deep bench of brand associations—including with Gucci, Nike, and Audemars Piguet—to show for it. But since her earliest days on the tour, she’s been a fiery on-court presence, known for her outbursts (she’s a world-class racket-smasher) and tense exchanges with officials. (She attributes much of this to being “very much a Taurus”).
Time and training have helped her to temper that fire—but it also has its uses. “This is part of my personality that can drive me crazy, but it can also drive me into that real fight mode and help me play with passion,” Sabalenka says. “It’s two sides of the medal.”
For Vogue’s May 2026 digital cover, @robertjhaskell meets Aryna Sabalenka in Rome to talk winning, losing, grieving, learning, and never letting up.
Photographed by @stevenkleinstudio,Written by @robertjhaskell, Styled by @tonnegood, Hair by @sandyhullett, Makeup by @markcarrasquillo, Manicure by @nailsbyetsuko, Tailoring by @sara.on.set.tailor, Produced by @thatoneproduction, Shot on Location at @faenamiamibeach.  Vogue, May digital cover 2026.

posts.caption: Summer is a season of subtraction, of revelation. In recent years, shorts have been getting smaller, shorter, and more abbreviated, and men like Donald Glover, Paul Mescal, and Harry Styles have embodied that seasonal thesis in thigh-revealing shorts. But a marked shift is underway—on the streets of major cities, on your social media feeds, on the runways, and perhaps even on your very own body. Short shorts are on the retreat. Big shorts are on the rise. “There’s something deeply American about them,” says designer Willy Chavarria, who often features shorts of Brobdingnagian proportions in his collections. Discover why bigger shorts are having a moment in menswear at the link in bio.
posts.alt_text: “It wasn’t until much later that I realised this place is what gave me my values. It’s my work ethic, it’s my belief in humanity, human rights — all of that I just folded into the work I do now,” said @NewYorkWilly of @WillyChavarria, explaining how his small hometown of Huron, California, shaped his work today, in a conversation with Vogue Runway and Vogue Business global director @NicolePhelps at the Vogue Business Global Summit in Chantilly. Tap the link in bio to read more on his global expansion plans, the hard launch of a handbag collection, and bringing the worlds of fashion and music closer together.

posts.caption:
On May 17, rumors broke that Shein had acquired Everlane. If it wasn’t officially confirmed three days later, it would read as satire: a brand that became popular for its commitments to radical transparency and sustainability is now under the umbrella of a brand known for its opaque practices and mass production. While the news was a shock to many customers who felt they had an accessible brand they could trust, Everlane struggled during the COVID-19 pandemic and failed to regain its footing. Private equity firm L Catterton took a majority stake in the brand in 2020; founder Michael Preysman stepped down in 2021, and leadership changes, layoffs, and debt followed.
At the link in bio, Preysman, who has spent the last year working on an electrolyte brand called Drink Magna, steps back into the world of fashion to address what happened in his own words, and reveal what he plans to do about it.
posts.alt_text: Photo by Vogue Runway on May 26, 2026. May be an image of magazine and text that says 'VOGUE RUNWAY う香 HOB00 EVERLANE FOUNDER MICHAEL PREYSMAN WEIGHS IN ON THE SHEIN SALE-AND REVEALS HIS NEW PROJECT'.

Sunny's inner glow illuminates her outer radiance, proving that true beauty is more than skin deep. Behind her dazzling smile lies a sharp mind, showcasing that beauty and brains can go hand in hand. With a heart of gold, her compassion shines as brightly as her career. Witness the multi-faceted charm of a woman who's not just a stunner, but also a thinker and a doer. Sunny Leone- redefining what it means to be a true icon.

posts.caption:
✨ Creator, Actor, PHENOMENON! ✨ On the June-July cover of Wedding Vows, we present the trailblazing @mostlysane, Prajakta Koli!
From building a YouTube empire to being the lead for Netflix Original Mismatched, from being part of Michelle Obama’s Day Time Emmy winning docu-series to being UNDP India's First Youth Climate Champion, her journey is nothing short of extraordinary.
With her debut romance novel set to launch this year, along with her first Amazon Prime Original Andhera, Prajakta’s journey is a testament to the power of dreams, hard work, and staying true to oneself. She continues to inspire millions with her authenticity, relatability, and unstoppable spirit.

posts.caption: Aishwarya looks beautiful yet there is no talk about it. Her saree look is very beautiful. But I don't like L'Oréal's ramp walk and these looks. Her clothes and hairstyle could have been better. She can be seen everywhere with the same open hair. And I find it very strange to have flying kisses all over again. It's okay once or twice, but I don't know why flying kisses over and over again is at least weird to me. There are many more poses. She was Miss World so it was very easy for her to pose, walk the ramp.

posts.caption:
@maudeapatow is ready now. Fresh off her return in the highly anticipated third season of #Euphoria, Apatow is stepping into a new chapter with the kind of clarity and control that only comes from growing up in real time—on-screen, off-screen, and alongside co-stars who have gone on to navigate intergalactic levels of fame and careers of their own. "Just thinking about us being little kids and having no idea what was in store to now… It's insane," she says.
In our May cover story, Apatow opens up about the roles that shaped her, the relationships that fulfilled her, the theater work that grounded her, and the rituals—travel, matcha, and all—that keep her moving forward. Read our full interview with the actress and director at the link in our bio.

posts.caption:
@lilamoss is having the time of her life. At just 23, Moss has already graced the cover of British Vogue, starred in a @marcjacobs beauty campaign, and walked for @coperni, @versace, @miumiu, and @victoriassecret. But her path into fashion wasn’t always a given, despite having a supermodel mother. “I was always really interested in fashion,” she says. “I was always putting on my mom’s shoes and doing fake runways. But it was never something really that my mom wanted me to do.”
From her very first modeling opportunity, Moss knew she had found her lane. Now, she’s using her platform to advocate for causes close to her heart—from proudly wearing her type 1 diabetes tech in editorials to collaborating on the first-ever type 1 diabetic Barbie. See how Moss is carving her own path in our summer cover story at the link in our bio.

posts.caption:
@jodiemcomer has never played it safe. ⁠
⁠
After captivating audiences as Villanelle in "Killing Eve," the actress is stepping into quieter, more complex territory with "The Death of Robin Hood." For WWD Weekend's latest cover story, Comer opens up about exploring darker creative spaces, embracing the projects that scare her most and why pushing boundaries still drives her. ⁠
⁠
Read the interview now at the link in bio.⁠
⁠

posts.caption:
This time around, @belmontcameli will be bringing fewer clothes. ⁠
⁠
The 28-year-old actor, in the midst of a major breakout thanks to Prime series @offcampusonprime, is still in New York wrapping up press for Season One at the time of this interview, and is due back on set in Vancouver in a mere five days to begin work on the second season. ⁠
⁠
“I’m so sick of going back to storage that I’ve just been traveling with two pairs of pants in one bag,” Cameli says. “Last year when I went to Vancouver, I brought way too much s–t — which ended up being fun because I hosted everyone on Friday nights [for] costume parties with all the clothes I had. But I say all of that to say, my life has become far more minimalist.”⁠
⁠
Read more at WWD.com — #linkinbio⁠
⁠
Report: @leighnordstrom

posts.caption:
On Tuesday night, Jeremy Scott saw his life laid out before him at the Longchamp boutique in SoHo.⁠
⁠
“This is [my] life in Le Pliage bags,” said the designer. “It’s pretty chic.”⁠
⁠
The designer was seated on the store’s second floor, where he was getting ready to host a party celebrating his 20-year collaboration with the French brand. Since 2006, Scott has put his distinctive mark on the Le Pliage bag, often featuring the prints that have defined each of his eponymous collections. The bags were displayed throughout the store, with a full spread of the collaboration exhibited on the top floor.⁠
⁠
Read more about the event at WWD.com — #linkinbio⁠
⁠
Report: @ktauer⁠
Photos: @lexieblacklock / WWD

posts.caption:
100 years after Route 66 brought America westward towards Hollywood glamour and the Pacific Ocean, the Regent Santa Monica Beach — perched on the golden coastline shores of Santa Monica — has become the destination point. Blending timeless elegance and modern luxury, the resort creates memorable experiences with its guestrooms, suites, elevated dining, wellness rituals and pool decks overlooking the Pacific Ocean. @regentsantamonica
Discover more at #linkinbio

posts.caption:
Moving swiftly to gird management, and foreshadowing a lifestyle push, @lanvin has appointed Barbara Werschine its new chief executive officer.⁠
⁠
Werschine was most recently CEO at cashmere specialist Bompard, where she “successfully modernized the brand and optimized its financial performance” over a four-year tenure, according to Lanvin.⁠
⁠
She has also served as director of leather goods collections at Hermès and held key leadership and development roles at Zadig & Voltaire, Celine and Louis Vuitton.⁠
⁠
Read more at WWD.com — #linkinbio⁠
⁠
Report: Miles Socha
posts.alt_text: Photo by WWD on May 28, 2026. May be an image of one or more people, makeup, magazine and text.

posts.caption:
@comitecolbert has touched down in New York, and celebrated on Tuesday evening with a cocktail party to kick off the opening of an exhibition celebrating the 250th anniversary of the U.S. ⁠
⁠
Located at The Shed, “Hidden Treasures, 250 Years of Franco-American Luxury Stories” features items from 65 French luxury brands that highlight the centuries-long special relationship between France and the U.S. ⁠
⁠
Guests including Pat Cleveland, J.Crew’s Olympia Gayot, Lizzie and Jonathan Tisch, Geoffroy van Raemdonck, Maye Musk, Olivier Bron and Pierre-Yves Roussel gathered on the top floor of The Shed for a glass of Veuve Clicquot, a ribbon-cutting ceremony and a chance to walk through the exhibition on its first day. ⁠
⁠
Read more at WWD.com — #linkinbio⁠
⁠
Report: @leighnordstrom⁠
Photos: @marsha_photo/WWD
